expectations.text: States isometrics can work but the most effective style is high-intent ballistic maximal contractions (max force as fast as possible)
expectations.evidence: Discusses isometrics as maintenance tools with 'high-tension holds' but does not specifically describe the most effective style as high-intent ballistic maximal contractions (trying to hit max force as fast as possible). Greg specifically emphasized this distinction.

expectations.text: Mentions strong angle specificity: gains transfer mostly to the trained joint angle, roughly 15-30 degrees in either direction
expectations.evidence: 'Angle-specific adaptation: Isometric strength gains are strongest within ~15° of the training angle.' Matches the expected ~15-30 degree transfer range.

expectations.text: Provides a practical example (e.g., pulling into safety pins at a chosen deadlift height)
expectations.evidence: Deadlift section: 'Can be performed with a barbell in a rack, against pins.' Mentions 'Off-floor position (1–2 inches)' and 'Mid-shin (mid-range)' as specific positions for pin work.

expectations.text: Notes that programming prescriptions (duration, frequency, weekly dose) are less standardized than conventional training
expectations.evidence: Actually provides specific programming as if well-established: '20–60 seconds at high tension, 3–5 sets per session, 2–3x per week.' Does not note that these prescriptions are less standardized than conventional training, which is what Greg emphasized.
